name: Vertex AI Platform Overview
description: Understanding the unified Vertex AI platform for building, training, deploying, and monitoring ML models with managed infrastructure on Google Cloud.
key_facts: Vertex AI unifies AutoML and custom training under one platform with managed notebooks, pipelines, and endpoints
Vertex AI Training supports TensorFlow, PyTorch, scikit-learn, and XGBoost with configurable machine types and GPU/TPU
Vertex AI Workbench provides managed Jupyter notebooks with GCP integration for data scientists
Custom containers in Vertex AI Training allow any ML framework packaged as a Docker container
Vertex AI Feature Store centralizes feature storage, serving, and versioning for consistent ML feature engineering
common_misconceptions: Vertex AI requires TensorFlow — it supports any ML framework via custom containers
Vertex AI Workbench replaces Colab — Workbench is enterprise-grade with GCP integration; Colab is for quick experimentation

name: AutoML for No-Code ML Model Training
description: Training production-quality ML models using AutoML Vision, Natural Language, Tables, and Video without writing ML code.
key_facts: AutoML Tables trains models on tabular data for classification, regression, and forecasting tasks
AutoML Vision trains image classification, object detection, and image segmentation models from labeled datasets
AutoML Natural Language trains text classification, entity extraction, and sentiment analysis models
Data quality is the primary determinant of AutoML model quality; minimum 1,000 labeled examples per class recommended
AutoML models can be exported to TensorFlow SavedModel or TFLite for on-device (Edge) deployment
common_misconceptions: AutoML produces inferior models compared to hand-crafted models — AutoML can achieve state-of-the-art results for many common tasks
AutoML requires no data preparation — data labeling, cleaning, and class balance are still critical for AutoML performance

name: Custom Model Training on Vertex AI
description: Scaling ML model training from prototype to production using Vertex AI Training with distributed training, hyperparameter tuning, and GPU/TPU acceleration.
key_facts: Vertex AI Training jobs use custom containers or pre-built containers (TF, PyTorch, scikit-learn) with configurable machine types
Vertex AI Hyperparameter Tuning (Vizier) automates optimal hyperparameter search using Bayesian optimization
Distributed training: data parallelism (same model, different data shards); model parallelism (different model parts on different devices)
Vertex AI Experiments tracks training runs, hyperparameters, and metrics for comparison and reproducibility
Vertex AI Training uses Cloud Storage for input data and model artifacts; use efficient data formats (TFRecords, Parquet)
common_misconceptions: More GPUs always means faster training — communication overhead between GPUs can reduce efficiency; use profiling tools to identify bottlenecks
Hyperparameter tuning must be done manually — Vertex AI Vizier automates this using intelligent search strategies

name: Model Serving and Online Prediction
description: Deploying ML models to Vertex AI Endpoints for real-time predictions with autoscaling, A/B testing via traffic splitting, and optimized serving containers.
key_facts: Vertex AI Endpoint hosts one or more deployed models; traffic splitting enables A/B testing and canary deployments
Serving container requirements: must accept JSON requests and return JSON predictions; custom containers extend this
Prediction autoscaling scales replicas based on prediction request rate; min-replicas prevents cold starts
Vertex AI Prediction supports TensorFlow SavedModel, PyTorch, XGBoost, scikit-learn, and custom containers
Feature store online serving provides low-latency (single-digit ms) feature retrieval for real-time predictions
common_misconceptions: Deploying a model to Vertex AI is the same as making it production-ready — production readiness also requires monitoring, A/B testing, and rollback plans
A model endpoint can only serve one model — endpoints support multiple deployed models with configurable traffic splits

name: Batch Prediction for Large-Scale Scoring
description: Running offline batch predictions on large datasets using Vertex AI Batch Prediction with BigQuery or Cloud Storage as input and output.
key_facts: Vertex AI Batch Prediction is cost-efficient for large datasets; does not require a running endpoint
Input sources: BigQuery tables or Cloud Storage files (CSV, JSONL, TFRecords); output to same source types
Accelerators (GPU/TPU) can be specified for batch prediction jobs to improve throughput
Batch prediction is managed: no need to configure workers; Vertex AI handles resource provisioning and cleanup
Explanations with batch prediction: feature attributions available for supported model types (TF, XGBoost)
common_misconceptions: Batch prediction is only for large jobs — it can also be used for periodic scoring jobs even if the dataset is small
Batch prediction requires an endpoint to be deployed — batch prediction jobs use model artifacts directly without a deployed endpoint

name: Vertex AI Pipelines for MLOps
description: Building and orchestrating ML workflows using Vertex AI Pipelines with Kubeflow Pipelines (KFP) SDK for automated, reproducible training pipelines.
key_facts: Vertex AI Pipelines runs Kubeflow Pipelines SDK or TFX pipelines on serverless GCP infrastructure
Pipeline components are containerized steps; pipelines define the DAG of component execution with data flow
Pipeline artifacts (datasets, models, metrics) are tracked in Vertex AI ML Metadata for lineage and reproducibility
Scheduled pipeline runs using Cloud Scheduler enable automated retraining on a cadence
Pipeline caching reuses outputs of unchanged components, reducing retraining cost for iterative development
common_misconceptions: Vertex AI Pipelines and Cloud Composer serve the same purpose for ML — Pipelines is optimized for ML workflows with ML Metadata; Composer is for general data engineering
Pipelines require KFP SDK — Vertex AI also supports TFX pipelines and TFDS for TensorFlow-native workflows

name: ML Model Monitoring and Data Drift Detection
description: Monitoring deployed ML models for prediction drift, feature drift, and model degradation using Vertex AI Model Monitoring.
key_facts: Vertex AI Model Monitoring compares production input distribution to training baseline; alerts on statistical drift
Feature drift (input distribution change) and prediction drift (output distribution change) are the two primary drift types
Skew detection: compares training data distribution to production serving data; drift detection compares prediction windows
Monitoring uses statistical tests (Jensen-Shannon divergence, Wasserstein distance) to quantify distribution shift
Model performance degradation (accuracy, F1) requires labeled ground truth from production; often collected with delayed feedback
common_misconceptions: A deployed model's accuracy stays constant over time — real-world data distributions change (concept drift) causing accuracy to degrade
Model monitoring only needs to be set up once — monitoring thresholds and baselines should be reviewed as data patterns evolve

name: Feature Engineering and Feature Store
description: Engineering ML features from raw data using BigQuery, Dataflow, and Vertex AI Feature Store for consistent, reusable feature serving in training and prediction.
key_facts: Vertex AI Feature Store provides online (low-latency) and offline (batch) feature serving from a unified store
Feature groups and feature views in Feature Store enable governance, reuse, and time-travel point-in-time lookups
Online vs offline consistency: Feature Store ensures training and serving use identical feature values, preventing training-serving skew
Dataflow transforms streaming events into features; BigQuery generates batch feature aggregations
TFX Transform component handles feature preprocessing with TensorFlow Transform, embedding the transformation graph in the saved model
common_misconceptions: Feature engineering is a one-time activity — feature engineering is iterative; new data patterns require new features
Training-serving skew is always a coding bug — it often results from using different preprocessing logic in training vs serving

name: BigQuery ML for SQL-Based Model Training
description: Training, evaluating, and deploying ML models directly in BigQuery using SQL without moving data, using CREATE MODEL and ML.PREDICT.
key_facts: BigQuery ML supports: linear/logistic regression, k-means, XGBoost, DNN, matrix factorization, ARIMA_PLUS forecasting, and imported TF models
CREATE MODEL statement trains a model; ML.EVALUATE assesses performance; ML.PREDICT scores new data
ARIMA_PLUS in BigQuery ML handles time-series forecasting with automatic seasonality detection and anomaly detection
Explainable AI with BQML: ML.EXPLAIN_PREDICT provides Shapley values for feature attribution
Export BigQuery ML models to Vertex AI for production-scale serving with autoscaling
common_misconceptions: BigQuery ML is only for simple models — it supports XGBoost and deep neural networks with feature transformations
BigQuery ML models can only run predictions in BigQuery — models can be exported to Vertex AI or TensorFlow for external serving

name: Responsible AI and Model Explainability
description: Implementing responsible AI practices including fairness evaluation, model explainability with Shapley values, and bias detection using Vertex AI tools.
key_facts: Vertex Explainable AI provides feature attributions using SHAP (SHapley Additive exPlanations) and integrated gradients
What-if Tool (WIT) enables interactive fairness analysis by slicing model performance across demographic groups
Model Cards document model purpose, performance, limitations, and ethical considerations for stakeholder transparency
Fairness metrics: demographic parity, equal opportunity, and predictive rate parity; each suitable for different use cases
Google's AI Principles: socially beneficial, avoid unfair bias, safety, accountability, privacy, scientific excellence, AI for all
common_misconceptions: Explainability means the model is fair — a model can be explainable but still biased; fairness requires separate evaluation
Higher accuracy always means a better model — accuracy can hide bias; disaggregate metrics by subgroup for fairness assessment

name: MLOps Maturity and Best Practices
description: Implementing MLOps practices to automate the ML lifecycle from data ingestion through model deployment and monitoring with CI/CD for ML.
key_facts: MLOps maturity levels: Level 0 (manual), Level 1 (automated training pipeline), Level 2 (CI/CD pipeline for ML)
Model registry in Vertex AI tracks model versions, metrics, and deployment history for governance and rollback
CI/CD for ML: trigger pipeline on data changes (Cloud Storage events), code changes (Cloud Build), or schedule (Cloud Scheduler)
Continuous Training (CT): automatically retrain models when data drift or performance degradation is detected
Shadow deployment: run new model in parallel without serving responses to users; compare predictions to validate before cutover
common_misconceptions: MLOps is just DevOps applied to ML — ML systems have unique challenges: data versioning, model drift, and experiment tracking not in traditional DevOps
Level 2 MLOps is necessary for all ML projects — start with Level 0 and automate incrementally based on team size and model count

name: GPU and TPU for ML Training and Serving
description: Selecting and configuring GPU and TPU hardware accelerators for deep learning training and serving workloads on Vertex AI and GKE.
key_facts: TPUs are Google-designed ASICs optimized for TensorFlow matrix operations; TPU v4 provides 275 TFLOPS per chip
Cloud TPU Node vs TPU VMs: TPU VMs provide direct VM access and are recommended for new workloads
GPU machine types: NVIDIA A100 (best for large model training), V100, T4 (inference-optimized)
TPU Pods enable multi-host training across hundreds of TPU chips with high-bandwidth interconnects
GPU preemptible instances offer significant cost savings for fault-tolerant training jobs with checkpointing
common_misconceptions: TPUs are always faster than GPUs — TPUs are optimized for dense matrix math in TensorFlow; sparse models or other frameworks may perform better on GPUs
You need a GPU for inference — T4 GPUs are cost-effective for inference, but many models run efficiently on CPU for moderate throughput

name: Generative AI with Vertex AI and Gemini
description: Building generative AI applications using Vertex AI Model Garden, Gemini API, fine-tuning foundation models, and RAG (Retrieval-Augmented Generation).
key_facts: Vertex AI Model Garden provides curated access to Gemini, Google's open models (Gemma), and third-party open-source models
Prompt engineering techniques: zero-shot, few-shot, chain-of-thought, and system instructions affect output quality
PEFT (Parameter-Efficient Fine-Tuning) methods: LoRA, prompt tuning adapt foundation models with less compute than full fine-tuning
RAG (Retrieval-Augmented Generation) grounds LLM responses in factual documents using vector embeddings and semantic search
Vertex AI Vector Search enables scalable semantic search over embeddings for RAG applications
common_misconceptions: Fine-tuning is always needed to customize LLM behavior — prompt engineering and RAG often achieve better results with less cost and effort
RAG eliminates LLM hallucinations — RAG reduces hallucinations by grounding in retrieved context, but models can still misinterpret retrieval

name: Data Preparation and Dataset Management for ML
description: Managing ML datasets using Vertex AI Dataset, data labeling with Vertex AI Data Labeling, and data versioning with Cloud Storage and BigQuery.
key_facts: Vertex AI Datasets store references to training data in Cloud Storage or BigQuery; datasets are versioned and shared across experiments
Vertex AI Data Labeling service provides human labeling for images, video, text, and audio through Google's labeler network
Data splits: training (60-80%), validation (10-20%), test (10-20%); stratified splits maintain class distribution
Class imbalance handling: oversampling (SMOTE), undersampling, class weights, or threshold tuning for minority classes
Data versioning with DVC (Data Version Control) or Vertex AI Experiments tracks dataset versions with model runs
common_misconceptions: More data always improves model performance — low-quality or mislabeled data degrades performance; quality > quantity
The test set can be used during development for tuning — using test data for any decisions leads to optimistic evaluation; keep it truly held-out

name: ML Model Evaluation and Selection
description: Evaluating ML model performance using appropriate metrics, confusion matrices, ROC curves, and cross-validation to select the best model for production.
key_facts: Classification metrics: Accuracy, Precision, Recall, F1, AUC-ROC, and PR-AUC; choose based on business requirements
Regression metrics: MAE, RMSE, R², MAPE; RMSE penalizes large errors more; MAE is more robust to outliers
Vertex AI Model Evaluation computes and stores metrics per model version for comparison in Model Registry
Cross-validation (k-fold) provides more reliable performance estimates than a single train/test split for small datasets
Business metric alignment: translate ML metrics to business KPIs (e.g., recall for fraud detection to catch rate)
common_misconceptions: Accuracy is the best metric for all classification problems — accuracy is misleading for imbalanced classes; use F1 or AUC
Higher AUC always means a better model in production — AUC is threshold-independent; evaluate at your operational threshold
